examples.title: Basic MapReduce Word Count Algorithm
examples.description: This pseudocode demonstrates the MapReduce programming model that was inspired by Google's 2004 paper and became the core processing paradigm in Hadoop 1.0
examples.code:
// MAP PHASE
FUNCTION map(line_number, line)
  words = split(line)
  FOR EACH word IN words
    emit(word, 1)  // Output: (word, 1)
  END FOR
END FUNCTION

// REDUCE PHASE
FUNCTION reduce(word, counts)
  total = 0
  FOR EACH count IN counts
    total = total + count
  END FOR
  emit(word, total)  // Output: (word, total_count)
END FUNCTION

// MAIN JOB
INPUT = "hdfs://input/data.txt"
OUTPUT = "hdfs://output/wordcount"

job = new MapReduceJob()
job.setMapper(@map)
job.setReducer(@reduce)
job.setInputPath(INPUT)
job.setOutputPath(OUTPUT)
hadoop.run(job)
examples.explanation: The MapReduce model has two phases: Map transforms input data into key-value pairs, and Reduce aggregates values with the same key. This word count example shows how parallel processing works in Hadoop, where each map and reduce task runs independently across the cluster.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of input records
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is the number of unique words

examples.title: Hadoop Streaming Word Count (Python)
examples.description: Python implementation using Hadoop Streaming API to demonstrate how non-Java programs can use Hadoop's MapReduce framework
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# mapper.py - Map phase
import sys

for line in sys.stdin:
    line = line.strip()
    words = line.split()
    for word in words:
        print(f"{word}\t1")

---
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# reducer.py - Reduce phase
import sys

current_word = None
current_count = 0

for line in sys.stdin:
    line = line.strip()
    word, count = line.split('\t', 1)
    count = int(count)
    
    if current_word == word:
        current_count += count
    else:
        if current_word:
            print(f"{current_word}\t{current_count}")
        current_word = word
        current_count = count

if current_word == word:
    print(f"{current_word}\t{current_count}")

---
# Command to run:
hadoop jar hadoop-streaming.jar \
  -input /input/data.txt \
  -output /output/wordcount \
  -mapper mapper.py \
  -reducer reducer.py
examples.explanation: Hadoop Streaming allows any executable (Python, Perl, Ruby) to serve as Mapper or Reducer. This demonstrates the flexibility that made Hadoop accessible to developers who were not Java experts, contributing to its widespread adoption.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is total input size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) for the shuffle/sort phase where k is unique keys
